p1.justification: The practice centers on establishing open-ended 'what if' spaces and inviting diverse residents to imagine different futures without prescribing a singular blueprint.

p1.verbatim_evidence: They particularly work with groups in frontline communities that are at the intersection of poverty, pollution and racism, with a focus on climate, economic and social justice.

p1.justification: The school represents an affirmative, material alternative to traditional educational environments, intentionally co-devised to transcend traditional blueprints and overcome the spatial and pedagogical alienation of standard classrooms.

p1.justification: The practice is built around a 'Possitopian' framework that combines facing the worst climate realities with imagining the best alternatives, aligning with critical dystopianism's integration of pessimistic warnings and active agency.
p1.verbatim_evidence: One of the principles of Climate Museum UK is to be ‘Possitopian’. This means both facing the worst and imagining the best, in ways that are both much more rational and critical and much more creative and open-minded.

p3.justification: The practice adopts a 'Possitopian' stance, which explicitly balances the pessimistic assessment of potential ecological collapse with the hopeful creation of alternative futures.

p1.justification: The practice articulates an affirmative and hopeful alternative to the routine, commercialized reality of the city, seeking to overcome urban alienation through shared, non-commercial public art experiences.

p1.justification: The practice is a performance of 'Urinetown', a cautionary future vision concerning water scarcity and societal control that utilizes Brechtian theater to prompt critical reflection on environmental and societal futures, featuring characters that drive the story's resistance.
p1.verbatim_evidence: using a Brechtian theatrical style to prompt reflection on environmental and societal futures... In particular, Djavan van de Fliert and Zoë Sleath who play the lead characters Bobby Strong and Hope Cladwell, are amazing.

p3.justification: The theatrical production acts as a dystopian warning about resource depletion, successfully instilling a conscious urge in the audience to conserve water in their daily lives.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Go see it, you’ll never leave the tap running while you clean your teeth again.

p3.justification: The production uses a Brechtian style to create intellectual distancing, prompting the audience to actively analyze and think about the issues rather than experiencing the performance as passive, purely emotional consumers.
p3.verbatim_evidence: The play is presented in the Brechtian style, with two characters... regularly stepping out of the flow of the story to tell the audience what is about to happen, and getting them thinking about what’s happening rather than just being passive consumers.

p3.justification: The practice utilizes pedagogical experiments and embodied experiences to help participants critically examine and deconstruct the status-quo structures of modernity.
p3.verbatim_evidence: to be very honest with you, it has been, in the experiments we’ve been doing with the collective, embodied experiences that make you get in contact with something that is in excess of knowing.

p3.justification: The collective leverages diverse perspectives to explore specific questions in relation to the imagination, provoking new ways of thinking beyond existing structures.

p1.justification: The Niyamgiri indigenous community's resistance represents an affirmative, holistic alternative to capitalist extractivism, valuing ancestral and sacred relationships with the mountain over monetary gain.
p1.verbatim_evidence: They said, “you can keep all your money. We’re not giving you our mountain. Our ancestors live in this mountain. It’s a sacred mountain.”

p3.justification: The tribal community exercises active hope and agency to resist corporate extractivism, prioritizing their sacred territorial connection and community flourishing over financial compensation.
p3.verbatim_evidence: They said, “you can keep all your money. We’re not giving you our mountain. Our ancestors live in this mountain. It’s a sacred mountain.”

p1.justification: The practice instantiates Concrete and Affirmative Utopianism by establishing a localized, community-led food market as a material and affirmative sociopolitical alternative that supports local businesses and fosters sustainable food systems.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Crystal Palace Transition Town in London created a new food market

p1.justification: The practice represents a pragmatic, localized, and community-funded energy initiative that implements the ecological visions of the Transition movement on a realistic scale.
p1.verbatim_evidence: emerged from Transition Bath and Transition Corsham... has now raised over £13 million in community investment from local people for renewable energy across the city

p3.justification: The project cultivates a forward-looking capability by mobilizing local citizens to invest in tangible green infrastructure, transforming ecological aspirations into active community-driven change.
p3.verbatim_evidence: has now raised over £13 million in community investment from local people for renewable energy across the city

p1.justification: The project instantiates a concrete and affirmative utopian vision by establishing a physical 'food belt' of microfarms to build a more resilient and hopeful food system.
p1.verbatim_evidence: aiming to create a ‘food belt’ around the city, a web of microfarms connected to the city

p1.justification: The practice is framed as a theatre production that opens up an imaginative space for audiences to bring the future into focus, aligning with open speculative futures.
p1.verbatim_evidence: ‘The Wedding’ matters because it creates one of those rare imaginative spaces where the future can come into focus.

p3.justification: The practice acts as an imaginative space that stimulates reflection and allows alternative visions of the future to be visualized and discussed.
p3.verbatim_evidence: ‘The Wedding’ matters because it creates one of those rare imaginative spaces where the future can come into focus.

p1.justification: The practice frames a complete societal collapse and loss of electricity as a catalytic rupture that demands participants entirely rewire and reconstruct human systems.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves

p2.justification: The practice uses imaginative 'what if' narratives, such as a zombie apocalypse scenario, within collaborative design sessions to generate practical off-grid technologies.
p2.verbatim_evidence: instead of saying, “Oh, how can we save electricity?”, they say, “Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves”. And then the ideas they come up with, they can then take into third world places

p3.justification: The practice utilizes playful speculative scenarios as a tool for imaginative provocation to unlock creative engineering capacities in digital native participants.
p3.verbatim_evidence: instead of saying, “Oh, how can we save electricity?”, they say, “Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves”.

p3.justification: The design sessions leverage the fictional crisis of a zombie apocalypse and loss of power as a rapid psychological catalyst to compel participants to redesign survival infrastructure.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves

p1.justification: The practice embodies a concrete, affirmative sociopolitical transformation where neighbors self-organize in district assemblies to influence municipal institutions and tackle issues like gentrification and tourism, offering a holistic alternative to top-down urban governance.
p1.verbatim_evidence: neighbours creating a civil society movement in order to influence the institutions as well.

p3.justification: The practice leverages a long-standing local tradition of active community self-organizing and collaborative action to drive societal transformation, cultivating a collective capacity to make positive changes.
p3.verbatim_evidence: In Barcelona there’s a long tradition of community movements. For a long time now people getting together to make changes.

p1.justification: The practice focuses on addressing actual, lived negative conditions like gentrification and drug tourism, framing these issues as realized sociopolitical crises in the local community.
p1.verbatim_evidence: at the moment, apart from the gentrification and tourism and so on, there’s a big problem with drugs for example, drug dealing and drug tourism. There’s many people from abroad coming here to take heroin.

p3.justification: The daily noise protest represents a persistent form of community resistance and agency that directly confronts the dark reality of local drug trafficking and gentrification.
p3.verbatim_evidence: every day at 10pm for example, neighbours go out to the window and do some noise... you just have to hit your saucepan, or another kitchen tool, and make noise... Just to complain.

p1.justification: The Superblocks represent a real, domesticated utopianism by implementing pragmatic, localized 'pilot initiatives' that experiment with 'new scenarios' for urban public space.
p1.verbatim_evidence: there are a couple of pilot initiatives in some districts which are closing the streets and creating some green parts... trying new scenarios for the public space. Like the superblocks.

p1.justification: The Fearless Cities Gathering is framed as a municipalist assembly focused on concrete transition and shared goals, representing a material, affirmative alternative to existing sociopolitical systems.
p1.verbatim_evidence: It was a municipalist gathering. ... Lots of workshops and roundtables about different topics. Common goals, transparency, Transition, communication... dynamic for sure.

p3.justification: The gathering generates a strong sense of collective energy, connection, and hope among international delegates, reinforcing their belief in a shared transformative alternative.
p3.verbatim_evidence: it gave you this energy, of feeling there’s quite a lot of people out there on the same page in different countries.

p1.justification: The practice represents an affirmative and material sociopolitical transformation where participants prefigured an alternative society through the direct, real-world experience of deep democracy in occupied squares.
p1.verbatim_evidence: For many people, those days in the squares with 15M were their first experience of deep democracy, of participation, of change.

p3.justification: Direct participation in the 15M assemblies served to foster active, educated hope by giving people a concrete, lived experience of democratic possibility and collective agency for change.
p3.verbatim_evidence: For many people, those days in the squares with 15M were their first experience of deep democracy, of participation, of change.

p1.justification: The practice represents a material, affirmative alternative to the alienation of global commodity food production by localizing agriculture, focusing on soil health, and introducing sustainable crops.
p1.verbatim_evidence: became fascinated by macro-nutrients, micro-nutrients, land use, what soil types there were around the city... increasingly understanding the global impact of commodity food production.

p3.justification: The project cultivates educated hope by offering open-source agricultural and culinary ideas to help the community build a sustainable, plant-based future without hoarding intellectual property.
p3.verbatim_evidence: We’re very open and we’re not interested in intellectual property. We’re interested in everyone taking these ideas and doing something with them.

p3.justification: It prompts imaginative exploration and community experimentation through collaborative storytelling and reciprocal recipe sharing, allowing participants to visualize new ways of eating.
p3.verbatim_evidence: We get sent so many recipes every week for things that people have done, that we had never imagined.

p1.justification: The practice represents a pragmatic, experimental real-world implementation of Transition Town ideas through a localized 9-acre Community Supported Agriculture initiative.
p1.verbatim_evidence: presented it to Transition City Norwich, as a chapter in their action plan, and they were really taken with it, and asked us to actually help them implement some of those ideas. So we set up a small CSA, 9 acres on the edge of the city

p1.justification: The practice represents an affirmative, material sociopolitical alternative grounded in localized agricultural production and cooperative economic development that counters conventional, alienated economic models.
p1.verbatim_evidence: working together, and cooperating, and collaborating to build a local economy.

p1.justification: The practice represents concrete and affirmative utopianism by using the Transition model to offer a hopeful, material, and community-based alternative to carbon-intensive lifestyles.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Over 1000 people were able to hear, and be inspired by, the Transition model who otherwise wouldn’t have been

p3.justification: The practice nurtures educated hope by inspiring individuals to identify and commit to proactive, localized actions for reducing emissions.
p3.verbatim_evidence: They were explicitly invited to think of concrete actions they might take in order to reduce their own emissions.

p3.justification: The practice adopts a critical transformative pedagogical orientation, urging listeners to move from awareness to active personal and collective agency to cut emissions.
p3.verbatim_evidence: please use it as the impetus to do something, to resolve, like I did in that cinema in 2006, to change something, to cut your emissions.

p1.justification: The Citizens' Assembly represents an affirmative sociopolitical transformation that serves as a concrete, hopeful alternative to the limitations of current political models.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Now that sort of transformation is unimaginable in the current political model... People who are part and parcel of these deliberative democratic processes... are living something... sheer energy and happiness of people who feel taken seriously.

p3.justification: The deliberative process generates a sense of collective empowerment, energy, and happiness among participants, nurturing hope in political possibilities previously thought to be unimaginable.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Now that sort of transformation is unimaginable in the current political model... People who are part and parcel of these deliberative democratic processes... are living something... sheer energy and happiness of people who feel taken seriously.

p3.justification: Participants undergo a transformation of consciousness by listening to diverse personal testimonies and expert information, enabling them to move past preconceptions and make decisions for the common good.
p3.verbatim_evidence: by sitting together with people, by listening to experts... listening to what has been sharing as testimonies... and now also on whether abortion should remain unconstitutional, and there too you see how citizens, although they lack competence, they have more freedom to make a decision for the common good

p1.justification: The Citizens' Assembly represents a pragmatic, real-world democratic experiment designed to bypass the divisions and limitations of traditional elections and referendums.
p1.verbatim_evidence: What they eventually did is they brought together a random sample of 300 citizens... This would not have been possible through elections and a referendum would have divided the community.

p3.justification: The practice emphasizes logical information gathering, objective questioning, and the rigorous cross-examination of technical evidence to reach a reasoned decision.
p3.verbatim_evidence: determine again, what are the questions we need to address? What is the information that is lacking? Who do we trust to give us that information? And on the basis of the information we get, and on cross-examination of that information, what sort of decision can we take?

p1.justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative sociopolitical transformation in local governance by establishing neighborhood assemblies and city-wide councils that elevate inhabitants from passive voters to active citizens, offering a concrete alternative to top-down political alienation.
p1.verbatim_evidence: interested in their inhabitants by listening to them... if politicians do not consider people just as voters, but as citizens.

p1.justification: The practice of municipal sortition instantiates a pragmatic, experimental 'real utopian' design by implementing the random selection of citizens to deliberate on local policy issues.
p1.verbatim_evidence: starting to draft people by lot. It’s cities like Utrecht, Rotterdam, Groningen... Inviting people to come and talk about clean energy, or migration policies

p3.justification: By randomly selecting citizens to deliberate on major public issues, the practice sparks discursive debate and reflection on societal values and alternative policies.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Inviting people to come and talk about clean energy, or migration policies, et cera.

p1.justification: The practice instantiates an open speculative future by rejecting a rigid, binary referendum in favor of an open deliberative process that explored a range of potential policy recommendations.
p1.verbatim_evidence: they tried to bring together a random sample of inhabitants of Great Britain to talk about this rather than to vote in a very rudimentary referendum

p3.justification: The practice prioritizes cognitive and logical processing of options by basing policy recommendations on deliberate dialogue, evidence, and information.
p3.verbatim_evidence: it shows that the conclusion might have been the same, but it was a much better buttress, and much better supported by evidence and information.

p3.justification: The practice leverages collaborative discussion and dialogue to provoke reflection on alternative sociopolitical directions rather than choosing from predefined paths.
p3.verbatim_evidence: to talk about this rather than to vote in a very rudimentary referendum.

p3.justification: The deliberation stretches the participants' political thinking and nourishes their opinions by exposing them to alternative viewpoints and options.
p3.verbatim_evidence: how their opinions have been nourished, and how their political thinking has been stretched beyond the horizon

p3.justification: Participants engage in a rational and cognitive process of changing their minds and recalibrating their stances when presented with new evidence and opposing viewpoints.
p3.verbatim_evidence: people change their minds as they are confronted with other viewpoints, new evidence, and they come to recalibrate their typical stand points

p1.justification: The practice embodies a concrete and affirmative alternative to standard commercial food production by prioritizing localized foraging and traditional methods over standard growth-oriented business models.
p1.verbatim_evidence: The modesty of his ambition affords him the ability to do things in a very different way than he otherwise would have to do as a commercial brewery.

p1.justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative, material alternative to conventional industrial brewing by implementing a radical, hyper-local, and holistic approach to sourcing ingredients and production, which actively works to overcome the alienation of modern mass production.
p1.verbatim_evidence: He’s taken his winemaking approach to beer making, but he’s gone to a radical extreme with getting barley producers in his area to grow different strains that he can use and floor malt himself.

p1.justification: Brewery Bhavana represents a concrete and affirmative utopianism where the founders express their imagination materially by creating a holistic, inclusive community space that combines a tasting room, dim sum, flower shop, and bookstore to overcome the alienation of typical craft beer environments.
p1.verbatim_evidence: they’re looking at the beer experience as the way that they’re going to express their imagination.

p1.justification: The practice instantiates a concrete and material alternative to conventional industrial agriculture by trialling carbon-positive, perennial cropping systems.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Their pursuit of carbon positive farming was really inspiring. They are trialling agroforestry with alley cropping... exploring perennial crops that could be grown.

p3.justification: The experimental efforts to develop carbon-positive and perennial agriculture serve as a source of inspiration and active, forward-looking hope for ecological transformation.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Their pursuit of carbon positive farming was really inspiring.

p1.justification: The Uppsala Climate Protocol represents an affirmative sociopolitical transformation in action, serving as a concrete, cooperative, and hopeful alternative to environmental inaction by committing key city organizations to reducing carbon emissions.
p1.verbatim_evidence: protocol signed by most of the key organisations in the city: businesses, municipalities, the university and others, which commits them to reducing their CO2 emissions.

p3.justification: The practice cultivates an orientation of imaginative exploration and social connection, prompting participants to step outside of their rigid professional roles to interact as creative and caring citizens.
p3.verbatim_evidence: speak to people not as people in their professional roles, but rather as people... who are imaginative and who care.

p1.justification: The practice represents an affirmative, grounded community transformation that seeks to actively overcome local socioeconomic alienation and build new possibilities.
p1.verbatim_evidence: to build new possibilities for themselves.

p3.justification: The project inspires forward-looking hope and agency by enabling participants to actively work towards generating new opportunities and overcoming local economic despair.
p3.verbatim_evidence: seeing what people are doing in that setting to build new possibilities for themselves.

p3.justification: The initiative cultivates radical imagination and community-led agency to dismantle economic exclusion, encouraging community flourishing and connection.
p3.verbatim_evidence: aim of Vivre Les Mureaux is to promote the area as a tourist destination... and to reimagine employment by creating a new, more diverse economy.

p1.justification: The initiatives in Grande-Synthe represent a concrete, material, and affirmative alternative to the industrial decay and poverty of the city, implementing holistic solutions like urban food forests, passivhaus housing, and organic school meals.
p1.verbatim_evidence: We visited some of the projects the Council have initiated: some passivhaus apartments, a repair shop, some fantastic Cuba-style apartments, the beginnings of a community-led urban food forest. The council has changed all the meals in schools to being organic. They have created better cycle networks, and offer residents €200 towards buying a bike.

p1.justification: The practice instantiates concrete and affirmative utopianism by establishing localized, physical creative spaces (Makerspaces) as material and hopeful alternatives that foster passion, engagement, and hands-on creation.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Here, In Pittsburgh where I am, there’s this really interesting push on Makerspaces. ... people are engaged and passionate about that, and they get excited, and they get to try new and different things, just by creating those spaces, and how important that is.

p1.justification: The practice functions as an affirmative and cooperative alternative to the alienation of modern adulthood, where play and creative spontaneity are typically suppressed, by providing a dedicated space for adults to reconnect and play.
p1.verbatim_evidence: I was intrigued by improv, and in taking some time and space to learn how, as an adult, to play again. As adults in 2017, we don’t play very much.

p3.justification: The practice leverages interactive storytelling and creative building exercises as a way to trigger collaborative imaginative exploration among participants.
p3.verbatim_evidence: We did lots of storytelling exercises. In one, you go round, each adding bits to a story... You build a chain where each suggestion is based on what the previous person has offered.

p3.justification: The training is designed to liberate the participants' creative energy and playfulness, helping them transition from passive, structured adult routines into active, imaginative agents.
p3.verbatim_evidence: it unlocks huge amounts of creative energy... finding depths of playfulness in yourself

p1.justification: The practice is rooted in the Transition movement, focusing on positive, collective, and nourishing actions that offer a hopeful, material alternative to build a better society together.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Confidence: that you are not alone, that together we can do something positive, nourishing and fun

p3.justification: The practice explicitly cultivates collective confidence and a belief in the ability to positively shape the future together, which represents hope as an active capability rather than naive optimism.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Confidence: that you are not alone, that together we can do something positive, nourishing and fun

p3.justification: The practice uses improvisational games to stimulate open-ended play and connected thinking, unlocking imaginative capacities to explore the unknown without being constrained by predetermined plans or expected results.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Pleasure: it's fun, it allows us to play with no objective or expected result... Connected thinking: the ability to make links between things that don’t appear to be connected.

p1.justification: The creation of the nature garden, which has grown into a small woodland, serves as a material, affirmative, and holistic alternative to conventional indoor primary school education, integrating ecological life and hands-on learning into the daily reality of the students.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Twenty years ago the school created a nature garden on a paddock next to the school. It is now like a small woodland... Every subject they study makes as much use of the garden as it can

p3.justification: The school employs an active, transformative pedagogy by fully integrating the nature garden into every school subject, moving beyond traditional, passive classroom-bound learning to cultivate active engagement with the environment.
p3.verbatim_evidence: She told us that the garden is used as a tool in every subject. 'If, in maths, we want to teach them to calculate circumference, we send them out to calculate the circumference of a tree... Every subject they study makes as much use of the garden as it can'.

p1.justification: The co-op represents an affirmative, material socio-ecological transformation that overcomes environmental degradation by reclaiming a heavily polluted sewage canal and restoring it to a natural state.
p1.verbatim_evidence: allowing the river to become a river once again.

p3.justification: The regional initiative demonstrates a forward-looking, active hope that rejects the permanent ecological destruction of the river, nurturing a collective belief that a highly degraded ecosystem can be healed.
p3.verbatim_evidence: allowing the river to become a river once again.

p1.justification: The Yellow House provides a physical and organizational base for Transition groups, representing a material and affirmative effort to build localized, hopeful alternatives to current ecological and social crises.
p1.verbatim_evidence: ‘Yellow House’ gallery and workshop provides a base for local Transition groups and related project.

p3.justification: The practice cultivates active, collaborative hope by bringing diverse community members together to actively design and implement local environmental projects and initiatives.
p3.verbatim_evidence: someone whose power to bring diverse people into a room together has led to many initiatives and projects.

p1.justification: The conference instantiates concrete, material efforts within the Transition movement, focusing on active projects and the development of local economic alternatives to build a hopeful alternative society.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Transition groups... international REconomy group to build an ‘ecosystem’ of REconomy practitioners.

p3.justification: The practice nurtures an active belief in alternative economic and social futures by bringing together practitioners to scale up and sustain localized projects.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Transition groups... international REconomy group to build an ‘ecosystem’ of REconomy practitioners.

p3.justification: The conference uses highly interactive, non-prescriptive formats designed to provoke reflection, debate, and social dreaming about alternative possibilities.
p3.verbatim_evidence: ‘Marketplace of Possibilities’, a lovely format, like World Café, where people with different projects sat at tables so people could go and chat with them.

p1.justification: The practice utilizes open-ended design thinking mindsets to encourage citizens to imagine and co-produce a shared vision of what a public museum can be, rather than implementing a rigid, pre-determined plan.
p1.verbatim_evidence: We use design thinking approaches, so human centred design mind-sets to define and understand, to think and imagine what might be

p3.justification: The design thinking and prototyping workshops focus on unlocking the public's imagination to visualize and experiment with alternative futures for the museum space.
p3.verbatim_evidence: We use design thinking approaches, so human centred design mind-sets to define and understand, to think and imagine what might be, and then to model and prototype that

p3.justification: The process transforms citizens and volunteers into active makers and shapers of their local museum, shifting them from passive consumers of heritage to active subjects who co-produce the space.
p3.verbatim_evidence: the expectations of the people that it’s not just us doing it, but we are doing it, together, for our collective good and for the good of our city, and our citizens

p1.justification: The event frames the future of the Silk Mill as an open, pluralistic space for public debate and social dreaming, asking a non-prescriptive 'what could we do here?' question.
p1.verbatim_evidence: a big blackboard that we drew out in the shape of the Silk Mill floor plan, with a, 'What could we do here?' question.

p2.justification: The practice functions as a participatory speculative design exercise, utilizing an interactive floor plan template to generate 'what if' scenarios and debate alternatives for the physical site.
p2.verbatim_evidence: a big blackboard that we drew out in the shape of the Silk Mill floor plan, with a, 'What could we do here?' question.

p3.justification: The event leverages imaginative exploration and the visualization of alternatives to provoke community debate and reflection on the potential future of the museum space.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Asking citizens to envision the space on a blackboard floor plan. ... a big blackboard that we drew out in the shape of the Silk Mill floor plan, with a, 'What could we do here?' question.

p1.justification: The practice conceptualizes the historical British boarding school system as a realized dystopian environment of extreme control, isolation, and brutality, treating it as an empirical historical reality rather than a speculative warning.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Everywhere else in Britain these people are regarded as war criminals. In this school they were neither forgotten nor processed.

p3.justification: The boarding school experience instills deep-seated, existential trauma and constant vigilance in children, producing long-term psychological damage and emotional repression.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Boarding School Syndrome, characterized by repressed trauma, inability to connect emotionally with others, and a severely limited or excised imagination.

p1.justification: The practice uses open-ended 'what if' questions to prompt community members to rethink and debate the future of their local urban environment, avoiding rigid blueprints in favor of exploring alternative possibilities.
p1.verbatim_evidence: One of the most powerful threads in Transition is “what would happen if?”... Rather than just asking “what would happen if this space were a green/square?” it gave people a taste of what that would be like, what it might feel like.

p3.justification: The practice focuses on unleashing the imagination of the community through a physical 'what if' installation, giving them a tangible experience of alternative futures to provoke public reflection and debate.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Rather than just asking “what would happen if this space were a green/square?” it gave people a taste of what that would be like, what it might feel like.

p1.justification: The practice is built around open-ended exploration of future possibilities through 'what if' questioning rather than a fixed utopian blueprint or predictive plan.
p1.verbatim_evidence: One of the most powerful threads in Transition is “what would happen if?”

p3.justification: The practice emphasizes unlocking the community's collective imagination and fostering social dreaming by asking speculative 'what if' questions.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Transition community spirit and imagining alternatives... One of the most powerful threads in Transition is “what would happen if?”

p1.justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative, material transformation of a public transit space, allowing the community to experience a hopeful, alternative use of the urban environment focused on community and sustainable transit.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Provided inspiration to reimagining the physical space of the bus circle

p3.justification: The practice provokes social dreaming and imaginative exploration of alternative urban futures by temporarily clearing a transit hub, allowing participants to experience and visualize the space differently.
p3.verbatim_evidence: the experience of seeing it through new eyes, inspired the idea for this year’s event.

p1.justification: The practice represents a material and affirmative community action that actively works to build local capacity and transcend feelings of helplessness, offering a hopeful alternative to current passivity.
p1.verbatim_evidence: During it, one person said “if we can do that, we can do anything”.

p3.justification: The project cultivates a sense of collective efficacy and learned hope, showing participants that active community collaboration can make alternative futures achievable.
p3.verbatim_evidence: Built significant community confidence and a sense of collective efficacy

p1.justification: The practice represents a concrete, material, and affirmative alternative to current food and environmental systems, using urban spaces to grow food and tackle climate change in a holistic and connected way.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Incredible Edible started as a conversation about climate change but it actually moved into a whole load of other things which are all connected.

p3.justification: The practice triggers critical consciousness and transformative conversations by prompting community members to question current food sources, local spaces, and the governance decisions behind them.
p3.verbatim_evidence: start a conversation about food. Where do we get our food from? ... start conversation about place ... And you can start a conversation about why are decisions being made in the way that they are?

p3.justification: By planting food in public spaces, the practice acts as an imaginative provocation that disrupts normal urban environments to spark public debate and reflection on alternative futures.
p3.verbatim_evidence: just by planting in public spaces, you can start a conversation about food.

p1.justification: The practice represents a material, affirmative sociopolitical transformation where a community reorganizes municipal buildings to address local service cuts and provide affordable housing.
p1.verbatim_evidence: in Wooler it was an issue of affordable housing, and it was an issue of local services being cut by the County Council. ... a local community development organisation came up with a plan which was basically to move various services in the town around ... freed up the former library space which was turned into affordable housing.

p3.justification: The practice encourages an open-ended, creative exploration of the future that avoids fixed blueprints, asking how to start in the present and see where it leads.
p3.verbatim_evidence: how people start to take their future in their own hands in a creative way, in a way that says, “Okay, let’s start here and see where it can lead?”

p1.justification: The Peace Gardens represent a concrete, material space reclaimed for public life and social interaction, deliberately structured to exist outside of direct monetization and capitalistic demands.
p1.verbatim_evidence: totally devoted to hanging out, to play, which is not about creating an immediate financial return.

p1.justification: The practice represents an affirmative sociopolitical transformation in a fragmented neighborhood, actively working to overcome isolation and alienation through community-driven art.
p1.verbatim_evidence: there was an old French man that lived there and didn’t have that many friends because he lost the way to connect with the youth... I remember him at the end of the day telling me, “I’ve never talked so much with the people around me in this neighbourhood. I’ve never had a connection with them.”

p3.justification: The practice uses public art to displace personal and historical images into new contexts, unlocking different meanings that provoke curiosity, storytelling, and reflection on community values.
p3.verbatim_evidence: It’s amazing to see sometimes what just a little bit of paper, a little bit of photography and image, sometimes enlarged a little, but replaced in a different context, can create a whole different meaning.

p1.justification: The mural represents a material, affirmative project designed to overcome community alienation and lack of belonging in a marginalized suburb by offering a highly inclusive and hopeful alternative where every segment of local society is represented.
p1.verbatim_evidence: in this neighbourhood, in the suburbs of Paris... to recognise that this place, that is forgotten, that is not heard, where the people don’t feel they belong to the French community. For me that’s one of the great ways to bring dignity back to people, by making them part of history.

p3.justification: The project cultivates a form of active hope and dignity in a tense, neglected neighborhood, counteracting alienation and despair by allowing residents to see themselves represented as a vital, proud part of history.
p3.verbatim_evidence: you should have seen how people were so proud to be part of this neighbourhood... For me that’s one of the great ways to bring dignity back to people, by making them part of history.

p1.justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative, community-driven alternative to commercialized art sponsorship, seeking to create a non-commodified project built and owned collectively by the people.
p1.verbatim_evidence: Create a chain of people so that we’re going to do that without any of those partners. We’ll want to use the energy that we’ve built over the years with all these communities, and to use them as promotion, and that I want to keep for what it is. For something that no-one can buy. And that the people own.

p1.justification: The practice focuses on physically manifesting a positive vision of the world through craft, framing it as an affirmative, compassionate alternative that empowers participants and helps overcome alienation, anxiety, and burnout.
p1.verbatim_evidence: my approach to Craftivism is ‘gentle protest’. You’re protesting against something but you always focus on what you want in the world, a positive vision of the world, and you’re using craft as a tool to do gentle protest which is careful, considerate and compassionate activism

p3.justification: The practice actively cultivates hope and a belief in the possibility of a better future by requiring participants to focus on positive visions of the world rather than just focusing on what they are protesting against.

p1.justification: The Transition Network acts as a concrete, affirmative utopian project that integrates collective imagination with active, material sociopolitical efforts to rebuild local communities.
p1.verbatim_evidence: much of what we do in Transition is about imagination, how to stimulate it, how to create places where people can come together to reimagine in order to then set about rebuilding.

p3.justification: The practice prioritizes unlocking and cultivating collective imaginative capacities and social dreaming to help participants visualize alternatives to current social realities.

p1.justification: The practice of banning public advertising represents an affirmative sociopolitical transformation aimed at reclaiming the public space, offering a concrete, hopeful alternative to highly commercialized urban environments.
p1.verbatim_evidence: freeing public space in Grenoble from advertising to develop areas for public expression

p3.justification: By replacing corporate advertising with areas dedicated to public expression, the city physically creates spaces that provoke community dialogue, reflection on shared values, and collective social dreaming.
p3.verbatim_evidence: freeing public space in Grenoble from advertising to develop areas for public expression

p3.justification: Removing omnipresent commercial advertisements that position citizens as passive consumers allows them to reclaim agency and participate as active subjects in shaping the community's public discourse.
p3.verbatim_evidence: freeing public space in Grenoble from advertising to develop areas for public expression

p1.justification: The practice of subvertising frames the reclamation of the 'attentional commons' as an active and transformative process of critique designed to dismantle the hegemonic, capitalist control of corporate advertising over public mental space.
p1.verbatim_evidence: actively reclaiming our mental environment ... Matthew B Crawford, in ‘The World Beyond Your Head’, talks about the need for an ‘Attentional Commons’.

p3.justification: The practice utilizes billboard interventions to unlock the creative imagination of community members, allowing them to visualize alternative uses of public space that provoke debate and raise local issues.
p3.verbatim_evidence: taking matters in their own hands, applying creativity, imagination, and a big dose of naughty to reclaiming our mental space... creating their own posters, and putting them up... work by local artists, raising local issues
